content:
# Acts Chapter 15 -- The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 243-244 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_015_page_001.jpg, ACT_015_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And certain-ones having-come-down from Judea were-teaching the brothers that, "If you-are not circumcised according-to the custom of-Moses, you-are not able to-be-saved."

**2** Having-become therefore no small dissension and debate by-Paul and Barnabas with them, they-appointed Paul and Barnabas and certain others from them to-go-up to the apostles and elders in Jerusalem concerning this question.

**3** They indeed therefore, having-been-sent-forward by the assembly, were-passing-through both Phoenicia and Samaria, recounting the conversion of-the Gentiles, and they-were-causing great joy to-all the brothers.

**4** And having-arrived in Jerusalem, they-were-received by the assembly and the apostles and the elders, and-they-reported-back as-many-things-as God did with them.

**5** But there-rose-up certain of-those from the sect of-the Pharisees having-believed, saying that it-is-necessary to-circumcise them and-also to-command [them] to-keep the law of-Moses.

**6** And the apostles and the elders were-gathered-together to-see concerning this matter.

**7** And much debate having-taken-place, having-stood-up Peter said to them, "Men, brothers, you yourselves know that from early days among you God chose through my mouth [for] the Gentiles to-hear the word of-the gospel and to-believe.

**8** And the heart-knowing God bore-witness to-them, giving the Holy Spirit just-as also to-us,

**9** and he-made-no-distinction between both us and them, by-faith having-cleansed the hearts of-them.

**10** Now therefore why are-you-testing God, to-place a yoke upon the neck of-the disciples which neither the fathers of-us nor we were-strong-enough to-bear?

**11** But through the grace of-the Lord Jesus we-believe to-be-saved according-to which manner also those [were saved]."

**12** And all the multitude became-silent, and they-were-listening-to Barnabas and Paul recounting as-many signs and wonders as God did through them among the Gentiles.

**13** And after the being-silent of-them, James answered saying, "Men, brothers, hear me.

**14** Symeon recounted how first God visited to-take out-of [the] Gentiles a people for-the name of-him.

**15** And with this the words of-the prophets agree, just-as it-has-been-written,

**16** 'After these-things I-shall-return and I-shall-build-again the tent of-David the-[one] having-fallen, and the-[things] having-been-dug-down of-it I-shall-build-again, and I-shall-set it upright,

**17** so-that the rest of-the men might-seek-out the Lord, and all the Gentiles upon whom my name has-been-called upon them, says [the] Lord doing these-things

**18** known from [the] age.'

**19** Therefore I judge not to-trouble the-[ones] from the Gentiles turning to God,

**20** but to-write to-them to-abstain from the pollutions of-the idols and from the fornication and from the strangled and from the blood.

**21** For Moses from generations of-old in every city has the-[ones] preaching him, in the synagogues being-read on every sabbath."

**22** Then it-seemed-good to-the apostles and to-the elders with the whole assembly, having-chosen men from them, to-send to Antioch with Paul and Barnabas -- Judas the-[one] being-called Barsabbas and Silas, men leading among the brothers --

**23** having-written through their hand: "The apostles and the elders, brothers, to-the-[ones] throughout Antioch and Syria and Cilicia brothers from the Gentiles, greetings.

**24** Since we-heard that certain-[ones] from us going-out troubled you with-words, unsettling the souls of-you, to-whom we-gave no-instruction,

**25** it-seemed-good to-us, having-become of-one-mind, having-chosen men, to-send to you with the beloved of-us Barnabas and Paul,

**26** men having-handed-over the souls of-them on-behalf-of the name of-the Lord of-us Jesus Christ.

**27** We-have-sent therefore Judas and Silas, and them through word reporting the same-things.

**28** For it-seemed-good to-the Holy Spirit and to-us no further burden to-place upon you except these necessary-things:

**29** to-abstain from idol-sacrificed-things and from blood and from strangled-things and from fornication; from which keeping yourselves, you-shall-do well. Be-strong."

**30** They indeed therefore, having-been-released, went-down to Antioch, and having-gathered-together the multitude, they-delivered the letter.

**31** And having-read [it], they-rejoiced at the encouragement.

**32** And Judas and Silas, being also themselves prophets, through much speech encouraged the brothers and strengthened [them].

**33** And having-spent time, they-were-released with peace from the brothers to the-[ones] having-sent them.

**34** [This verse is absent in Sinaiticus.]

**35** And Paul and Barnabas were-spending-time in Antioch, teaching and proclaiming-good-news the word of-the Lord, with also many others.

**36** And after certain days, Paul said to Barnabas, "Having-returned indeed, let-us-visit the brothers in every city in which we-proclaimed the word of-the Lord, how they-are-having."

**37** And Barnabas was-wishing to-take-along also John the-[one] being-called Mark.

**38** But Paul was-considering-worthy, the-[one] having-departed from them from Pamphylia and not having-gone-with them to the work, this-[one] not to-take-along.

**39** There-came-about therefore a sharp-contention, so-as them to-be-separated from one-another, and-Barnabas having-taken-along Mark to-sail-away to Cyprus,

**40** and-Paul having-chosen Silas went-out, having-been-handed-over to-the grace of-the Lord by the brothers.

**41** And he-was-passing-through Syria and Cilicia, strengthening the assemblies.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**Acts 15 -- The Jerusalem Council and the Apostolic Decree**

1. **Verse 2 -- "no small" (ouk oligon):** Sinaiticus reads the characteristic Lukan litotes "no small dissension and debate" (staseos kai zeteseos ouk oliges). The double genitive construction emphasizes the severity of the conflict.

2. **Verse 7 -- "from early days" (aph hemeron archaion):** Sinaiticus preserves this reading. Peter's reference is to the Cornelius episode of Acts 10, placing the Gentile question as already divinely settled.

3. **Verse 11 -- "according to which manner also those":** The Greek leaves the comparison deliberately compressed. Peter's argument equates the salvation mechanism: Jewish believers are saved by grace in the same way Gentiles are -- not the reverse order one might expect.

4. **Verse 12 -- Order "Barnabas and Paul":** Sinaiticus preserves the order with Barnabas first, reflecting the Jerusalem perspective where Barnabas was the more familiar figure. Contrast with v.2 where "Paul and Barnabas" reflects the Antiochene perspective.

5. **Verse 14 -- "Symeon" (Sumeon):** Sinaiticus uses the Hebraized form of Simon/Peter's name, consistent with James's Jewish-oriented speech at the Council.

6. **Verses 16-18 -- Amos 9:11-12 (LXX):** The quotation follows the Septuagint form, which differs markedly from the Hebrew Masoretic Text. The LXX reads "that the rest of men might seek" where the MT has "that they may possess the remnant of Edom." James's argument depends on the LXX reading.

7. **Verse 20 -- "strangled" (pnikton):** Sinaiticus includes the fourfold decree: idol-pollutions, fornication, strangled-things, and blood. The Western text tradition (Codex Bezae) omits "strangled" and adds a negative Golden Rule, transforming the decree from ritual-dietary to purely ethical.

8. **Verse 23 -- Letter salutation:** The greeting formula "chairein" (greetings/rejoicing) is identical to the secular Greek letter opening, matching the form in James 1:1 -- a notable connection given James is the speaker.

9. **Verse 24 -- "to whom we gave no instruction":** Sinaiticus lacks the additional phrase found in some manuscripts ("commanding to be circumcised and keep the law"). The shorter reading leaves the false teachers' specific content unspecified in the letter itself.

10. **Verse 29 -- Decree formulation:** Sinaiticus presents the four prohibitions as: eidolothyton (idol-sacrificed things), haima (blood), pnikton (strangled things), porneia (fornication). This Alexandrian text-type fourfold form is widely regarded as original.

11. **Verse 34 -- Absent:** Sinaiticus omits verse 34 entirely ("But it seemed good to Silas to remain there"). This verse is a later scribal addition found in some manuscripts (notably Western witnesses) to explain how Silas was available in v.40. The omission is original.

12. **Verse 39 -- "paroxysmos" (sharp contention):** The Greek paroxysmos (whence English "paroxysm") denotes a violent eruption of feeling. Luke does not soften the conflict between Paul and Barnabas. The result clause with hoste + infinitive indicates a complete separation.

13. **Verse 40 -- "having been handed over to the grace":** The passive participle paradotheis implies the church's formal commissioning of Paul (but not Barnabas), possibly suggesting Luke's subtle narrative judgment on who held the right position in the dispute.

-- EMET
